chapters.title: Lincoln's rivals and what they stood for
chapters.text:
Abraham Lincoln served as America's sixteenth president from 1861 until his assassination in 1865. A lawyer and politician, he guided the nation through civil war and helped bring slavery to an end.

When Lincoln sought the presidency in 1860, his chief competitors were Edward Bates, Salmon Chase, and William Seward. Doris Goodwin shows that Lincoln treated these men with genuine regard rather than contempt. He privately believed Seward, a leading Republican senator, might be the stronger candidate — and that judgment had real support. Voters admired Seward for taking an active interest in their welfare and stepping in when they faced hardship. Democrats, too, saw him as a natural statesman capable of leading and advising. Newspapers on both sides treated his nomination as almost inevitable.

Chase, Ohio's representative, impressed observers as a disciplined, statuesque public figure whose associates described him as controlled and serious. A committed abolitionist, he grounded his opposition to slavery in Christian morality and became a pillar of the Republican cause. Chase considered himself the rightful heir to the presidency and was so certain of his moral mission that he barely mounted a conventional campaign.

Bates projected a different image: more devoted family man than hungry office-seeker. He and his wife dressed plainly and avoided the trappings of ambition. In his diary, Bates wrote gratefully about his children and spouse, calling them divine gifts. Still, he cared deeply about the country's direction and wanted to halt the spread of slavery.

What qualities made Lincoln so appealing to so many Americans? Part of the answer lies in how he handled rivalry. Greatness, Goodwin suggests, includes honoring opponents rather than diminishing them. Charisma paired with dedication can form a personality that draws others in — and Lincoln modeled that combination long before he reached the White House.

chapters.title: How Lincoln ran — and how his rivals stumbled
chapters.text:
During the 1860 race, Lincoln worked quietly to build a reputation that would resonate nationwide. He disliked flashy talk about "power," yet his actions showed unmistakable resolve. When voters discussed the coming election, he expressed only measured enthusiasm about his competitors — but anyone watching his conduct could see he intended to win. While Seward's backers ran a polished operation, Lincoln largely promoted himself. Even when Jesse Fell of the Illinois Republican committee urged him to court the press, Lincoln rejected empty praise and manufactured publicity.

Goodwin points to critical missteps by his rivals. Seward's reputation for radicalism led advisers to send him on an eight-month tour of Europe, fearing he might speak too bluntly on slavery before voters were ready. He returned softer and less confrontational, and his comeback speech won applause. Confident of victory, his team then clashed with Horace Greeley of the New York Tribune. Greeley undermined Seward by boosting Bates, which unexpectedly raised Bates's profile.

Chase mishandled his written campaign by leaning on politician Hiram Barney instead of hiring a skilled manager, and he alienated useful allies in the press. Despite his antislavery credentials, he neglected how he appeared to ordinary voters.

Self-confidence helps until it distorts one's self-perception.

Bates, meanwhile, seemed out of touch with national tension. His devotion to home life left him slow to grasp how deeply slavery was dividing the country. He argued that leaders should prioritize economic questions over moral ones about bondage, treating the slavery debate as too explosive for any party to handle well. Republicans fighting for emancipation heard that as a betrayal of their cause.

chapters.title: Victory, grace, and building a cabinet of enemies
chapters.text:
The Republican convention met in Chicago in 1860. Seward and Lincoln emerged as the two strongest contenders, backed by party activists and the public. When Lincoln won, his defeated rivals accepted the outcome with outward dignity — though Bates privately recorded his frustration. Rumors claimed Lincoln had won through trickery; he himself received the news with quiet composure. Witnesses said he appreciated congratulations but seemed eager to go home and share the moment with his wife.

Historians have long asked how Lincoln prevailed. Seward carried as many detractors as admirers, and his footing looked less secure than that of Lincoln, a comparatively obscure figure whom few rivals bothered to attack. Lincoln also occupied the pragmatic center of the party — less conservative than Bates, less radical than Chase and Seward. He captured the presidency on November 6, 1860.

Having risen to power with fewer privileges than any of his rivals, Lincoln was more accustomed to rely upon himself to shape events. ~ Doris Goodwin

Forming a cabinet forced hard choices. Bates, Seward, and Chase topped the list. Lincoln gave the most prestigious post, Secretary of State, to Seward. Bates accepted the Attorney Generalship, saying he could no longer focus only on family while the nation fractured. Chase initially refused the Treasury because he would not serve as a subordinate, though he offered general support. Lincoln persisted, and Chase eventually agreed.

Friends can help in complex situations, yet you should always be capable of helping yourself.

chapters.title: From inaugural conviction to civil war
chapters.text:
What else made Lincoln remarkable? Shortly after taking office he visited Philadelphia to speak about freedom, invoking the 1776 Declaration of Independence and its promise of equality. Slavery still contradicted that promise, and enslaved Americans knew it. Lincoln insisted that justice favored their cause and that the Declaration offered hope of eventual equality. He said he would rather die than abandon its principles.

That remark carried grim foreshadowing. His security chief, Allan Pinkerton, uncovered an assassination plot and urged Lincoln to leave Philadelphia. The president refused, arguing that his duty to the public outweighed personal risk. He was staking his life on his convictions.

By 1861 sectional hostility erupted into civil war. Southern states dependent on enslaved labor rejected the Union's antislavery direction and formed the Confederate States of America. The North held Kentucky, North Carolina, Tennessee, and Virginia; Confederates celebrated capturing wealthy Virginia.

Washington, D.C., the Union capital, was cut off from northern supply lines. Confederates severed telegraph wires, and residents feared the city might fall.

Wartime grief struck Lincoln personally when his son William died of typhoid fever linked to polluted water in the Potomac, where soldiers dumped waste. Mary Lincoln never recovered from the loss and held spiritualist séances in the White House, hoping to contact William's spirit.

Hard times pushed many toward reconciliation. Public figures suffer private losses too, yet they must remain steady.

Did you know? Lincoln believed the dead endure as long as the living remember them.

chapters.title: Turning the tide toward emancipation
chapters.text:
Lincoln believed morale visits to soldiers outside Washington were essential. On June 20, 1864, he traveled to Virginia to meet General Ulysses S. Grant, whom he had placed in supreme command. Lincoln praised Grant's strategy for reclaiming Confederate-held territory.

Union forces recaptured Atlanta that August. Lincoln ordered a hundred-gun salute in Washington and other cities to honor the victory. Citizens took heart, believing the war might finally end on northern terms. Yet another presidential election loomed, with Democrats nominating General George McClellan. Lincoln needed reelection to finish his central aim: freedom for the enslaved. His policy served the nation's future, but not every voter agreed.

Democrats expected McClellan to prevail. Lincoln cultivated a different constituency — the troops themselves. After defeats he visited wounded men, shook hands, and wished them recovery. His humor and storytelling spread through camps. He understood the sacrifice they were making. Soldiers repaid that regard: roughly eight in ten cast ballots for him, securing a second term.

Over the years, Lincoln had inspired an almost mystical devotion among his troops. ~ Doris Goodwin

By January 1865, Lincoln anxiously pushed the Thirteenth Amendment banning slavery, fearing Confederate sabotage in Washington. The measure passed 119 to 56, and the chamber erupted in cheers.

Fight to the last for beliefs that benefit humanity.

chapters.title: Richmond, reunion, and assassination
chapters.text:
Lincoln wanted the killing to stop before more lives were lost. He also proposed compensating formerly enslaved people, an idea several congressmen resisted. Some politicians worried that peace overtures might provoke violence against the president. Senator William Fessenden argued that compensation could wait until fighting ended; diverting more resources from the army might prove ruinous.

In early April 1865, Union troops seized Petersburg, Virginia. Grant captured twelve thousand prisoners and drove Confederates from the city. Soon afterward General Godfrey Weitzel took Richmond. Seward grew alarmed for Lincoln's safety, suspecting defeated rebels might target the president.

On April 4 Lincoln entered Richmond and saw devastation everywhere — wreckage, dead horses, unexploded ordnance. Freed people knelt before him, calling him a messiah. Characteristically modest, he told them to thank God, not him, for emancipation.

Modesty keeps leaders grounded among those they serve.

Unlike a hardened general, Lincoln mourned each casualty and longed for peace.

Confederates surrendered on April 9, ending the war. Grant returned to Washington on April 13 amid plans for celebration. The Lincoln family rejoiced that bloodshed had ceased. On April 14 Grant visited the White House to discuss reconstruction laws. Mary Lincoln recalled her husband's unusual cheerfulness that day. But actor John Wilkes Booth plotted killings of Lincoln, Seward, and Vice President Andrew Johnson. Booth wounded Seward at home and failed to reach Johnson. At Ford's Theatre he shot Lincoln in the head. The nation grieved a leader whose modesty, persistence, and moral courage had reshaped the country.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Antislavery sentiment ran strong in the Republican Party, yet few members matched Lincoln's tireless, selfless pursuit of liberation. He embodied a rare blend of humility, steadiness, and principle. He won office without trashing rivals to elevate himself. Instead he saw talent in opponents and recruited Seward, Chase, and Bates into a cabinet built on mutual respect. Seward, especially, watched over Lincoln's welfare.

When North and South split, Lincoln understood that ending slavery would demand even greater resolve. Historians rank the resulting conflict among America's bloodiest struggles. His leadership nevertheless produced Union victory and lasting social change. Enslaved people gained freedom in practice, not merely in rhetoric, and remembered his restraint with gratitude. Hatred and moral blindness ultimately cost him his life, yet he remains one of the republic's most admired statesmen.

Try this

Study other influential political leaders and note what their careers teach you. Watch the documentary "Lincoln" to observe how the president conducted himself; the performances capture distinctive nineteenth-century personalities.

Read it till the end?
